coredata.dc:title: Photocatalytic activity tuning in a novel Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite: Synthesis and photocatalytic mechanism
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Huajing
Wang, Fei
Wang, Shifa
Wang, Xiangxian
Yi, Zao
Yang, Hua
coredata.dc:description: CuBi2O4 dumbbell-like architecture built from nanorods was synthesized by a simple coprecipitation route. The growth process of the dumbbell-like architecture was investigated by varying the reaction duration time. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and Ag2S nanoparticles were assembled on the surface of the CuBi2O4 dumbbell-like architecture to produce CQDs/CuBi2O4 and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composites. Compared to bare CuBi2O4, the composites, particularly Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4, manifest an increased visible light absorption, increased photocurrent density and decreased charge-transfer resistance. Using simulated sunlight as the light source and rhodamine B (RhB) dye as the target organic pollutant, the photocatalytic activity of the samples was investigated. It is demonstrated that the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite exhibits the highest photocatalytic activity toward the RhB degradation. This is due to the highly efficient separation of electron/hole pairs and increased visible light absorption of the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CuBi2O4 dumbbell-like architecture
CQDs
Ag2S nanoparticles
Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite
Photocatalytic activity

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical route for the preparation of CuBi2O4, CQDs/CuBi2O4, and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4.
XRD patterns of CuBi2O4 samples obtained at different reaction times.
SEM images of CuBi2O4 samples obtained at different reaction times. (a) 0.5 h; (b) 1 h; (c) 7 h; (d) 12 h.
Growth process of dumbbell-like CuBi2O4 architecture and formation mechanism of CQDs/CuBi2O4 and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composites.
XRD patterns of the CuBi2O4, CQDs/CuBi2O4, and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 photocatalysts.
FTIR spectra of the CuBi2O4, CQDs/CuBi2O4, and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 photocatalysts.
Raman spectra of the CuBi2O4 and CQDs/CuBi2O4 photocatalysts.

(a, b) TEM images, (c) HRTEM image, (d) DF-STEM image, and (k) EDS spectrum of the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite. The inset in (b) shows the SAED pattern of the composite. (e)-(j) The corresponding elemental mapping images of the region indicated in (d).
N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm and pore size distribution curve (inset) of the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite.

(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the CuBi2O4, CQDs/CuBi2O4, and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 photocatalysts. The inset in (a) shows the corresponding first derivative curves of the spectra. (b)‒(d) The real photos of CuBi2O4, CQDs/CuBi2O4, and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4, respectively.
(a) XPS survey scan spectrum, (b) Cu 2p spectrum, (c) Bi 4f spectrum, (d) O 1s spectrum, (e) C 1s spectrum, and (f) Ag 3d spectrum of the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite.
(a) Photocurrent response curves and (b) EIS spectra of the CuBi2O4, CQDs/CuBi2O4, and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 photocatalysts.

(a) Time-dependent photocatalytic degradation of RhB over the CuBi2O4, CQDs/CuBi2O4, and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 photocatalysts under simulated sunlight irradiation. (b) Plots of ln(C/C0) vs irradiation time for the photocatalysts. (c) Recyclability of the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 photocatalyst for the photocatalytic degradation of RhB dye under simulated sunlight irradiation. (d) Effect of ethanol, BQ and AO on the degradation of RhB over the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 photocatalyst under simulated sunlight irradiation.
A possible photocatalytic degradation mechanism of the RhB dye over the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 photocatalyst.
Color coordinates and E g values of the CuBi2O4, CQDs/CuBi2O4, and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Photocatalytic activity tuning in a novel Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite: Synthesis and photocatalytic mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Processing and Recycling of Non-Ferrous Metals, Lanzhou University of Technology, Lanzhou 730050, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Processing and Recycling of Non-Ferrous Metals

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Joint Laboratory for Extreme Conditions Matter Properties, Southwest University of Science and Technology, Mianyang 621010, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Joint Laboratory for Extreme Conditions Matter Properties
Southwest University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electronic and Information Engineering, Chongqing Three Gorges University, Chongqing, Wanzhou 404000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electronic and Information Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CuBi2O4 dumbbell-like architecture built from nanorods was synthesized by a simple coprecipitation route. The growth process of the dumbbell-like architecture was investigated by varying the reaction duration time. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and Ag2S nanoparticles were assembled on the surface of the CuBi2O4 dumbbell-like architecture to produce CQDs/CuBi2O4 and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composites. Compared to bare CuBi2O4, the composites, particularly Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4, manifest an increased visible light absorption, increased photocurrent density and decreased charge-transfer resistance. Using simulated sunlight as the light source and rhodamine B (RhB) dye as the target organic pollutant, the photocatalytic activity of the samples was investigated. It is demonstrated that the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite exhibits the highest photocatalytic activity toward the RhB degradation. This is due to the highly efficient separation of electron/hole pairs and increased visible light absorption of the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalysis is an effective process used to remove organic pollutants from dye wastewater including congo red, methylene blue, methyl red, methyl orange, acidic scarlet-18, rhodamine B, and so on [1–6]. In this process, photocatalysts play a key role for the degradation of organic pollutants by producing electron/hole (e−/h+) pairs. Titanium dioxide (TiO2) and zinc oxide (ZnO) are two kinds of important photocatalysts for the degradation of organic dyes under ultraviolet (UV) irradiation [7,8]. However, the large optical bandgap (E g) values limit their application in the visible light range sunlight. Furthermore, the photon utilization rate for bare photocatalysts is very low because the photoexcited electrons and holes easily undergo rapid geminate recombination. In order to promote the practical application of photocatalysts with the best use of solar energy, two different routes have been generally considered: (1) modification of the photocatalysts (e.g., decoration with noble metals, surface disorder engineering, assembly with carbon materials and construction of heterojunction composites) [9–15] and (2) development of novel visible-light photocatalysts [16–19].

In recent decades, spinel oxides with AB2O4 structure have been widely studied due to their high photocatalytic activity, high-temperature heat capacity, high thermal and chemical stability, high mechanical resistance, fascinating photoelectric properties, and potential applications in pigments, catalysts, sensors, lightweight armors, microwave devices and magnetic storage systems [20–24]. Copper bismuth oxide (CuBi2O4) is a typical spinel oxide, and possesses strong visible light response, large light penetration depth, excellent photo-stability. Due to these outstanding properties, CuBi2O4 can be promisingly used as a visible-light photocatalyst and photoelectrode material [25–28]. However, CuBi2O4 exhibits a poor charge carrier mobility and high electron/hole recombination. Very recently, Wang et al. demonstrated that the decoration of AuAg alloy nanoparticles on CuBi2O4 hierarchical microcuboids hold a promising potential to enhance the photocatalytic activity of CuBi2O4 [29].

Low-dimension nanomaterials like graphene, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), metal nanoparticles and SiC nanowires offer great potential applications for biological labeling, bioimaging, drug delivery, energy conversion, optoelectronic devices, wave absorption and sensors due to their attractive properties [30–41]. In particular, photoexcited CQDs are excellent electron donors and electron acceptors, which makes CQDs a good photocatalyst modifier to promote the separation of photogenerated electron/hole pairs in photocatalysts. Several examples of CQDs-modified photocatalysts with enhanced photocatalytic performance can be found in the composite systems of CQDs/Bi2WO6, α-Bi2O3/C-dots, CQDs/Cu2O and CQDs/Ag3PO4 [42–45]. Silver sulfide (Ag2S), well known as a narrow bandgap semiconductor with E g ≈ 1.0 eV, has a strong and broad absorption in the visible and near-infrared region of solar spectrum. Due this promising property, Ag2S has been widely used as a co-catalyst to modify other semiconductor photocatalysts, such as Ag2S/Bi4Ti3O12, Ag2S/TiO2, NaNbO3/Ag2S, Bi2WO6/Ag2S, ZnO/Ag2S, and Ag2S/BiFeO3 [46–51]. These Ag2S-based composite photocatalysts have been shown to exhibit obviously enhanced photocatalytic performances. The main reasons can be attributed to the enhanced visible/near-infrared light absorption of the composites and the highly efficient separation of photogenerated electron/hole pairs resulting from the carrier transfer between Ag2S and other semiconductors. Inspired by the above information, the decoration of CQDs and Ag2S nanoparticles on CuBi2O4 is expected to achieve an excellent Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite photocatalyst with enhanced photocatalytic activity. To date, however, there has been no work performed on the preparation and photocatalytic activity of the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite photocatalyst.

In this work, CuBi2O4 dumbbell-like architecture was synthesized via a simple coprecipitation route, and its growth process was elucidated through different reaction times. CQDs were decorated on the CuBi2O4 dumbbell-like architecture to form CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite. Ag2S nanoparticles were assemble on CQDs/CuBi2O4 to produce Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite. The color, optical, and electrochemical properties the as-prepared CuBi2O4, CQDs/CuBi2O4, and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 photocatalysts were studied by various characterization methods. The photocatalytic activity of the samples was evaluated by the degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) dye under simulated sunlight.

CuBi2O4 photocatalyst was synthesized via a simple coprecipitation route. In a typical synthesis procedure, 2 mmol of Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, 4 mmol of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and 5 mmol of KCl were successively dissolved in 20 mL of dilute nitric acid solution (2 mL HNO3 + 18 mL distilled water) to form a uniform mixture solution. 9.6 g of NaOH was dissolved in 60 mL of distilled water, which was then dropped slowly into the above mixture solution under constant stirring, during which time a suspension was immediately formed. In this experiment, KCl was used to adjust the surface morphology of CuBi2O4 photocatalyst [29]. The suspension was magnetically stirred for another 12 h at a temperature of 15 °C. The produced precipitate was collected and washed with distilled water (3 times) and ethanol (2 times), followed by drying at 60 °C for 12 h. The final product was obtained as CuBi2O4 photocatalyst. To reveal the effect of reaction time on the morphology of CuBi2O4, a series of CuBi2O4 samples were prepared by varying the reaction time at 0.5, 1, 3, 7, and 12 h. The flow chart for the preparation of CuBi2O4 photocatalyst via the coprecipitation route is shown schematically in Fig. 1 (I).

CQDs were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method using glucose as the carbon source. 1 g of glucose was added to 80 mL distilled water, followed by ultrasonic treatment for 30 min and magnetic stirring for another 30 min. The glucose solution was then loaded into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (100 mL) and subjected to heat treatment at 180 °C for 4 h. After the autoclave was naturally cooled to room temperature, the solution was filtered through a millipore filter paper two times. The obtained reddish-brown solution was CQDs suspension. To prepare CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite, 6 mL of the CQDs suspension was added in 20 mL of dilute nitric acid solution (2 mL HNO3 + 18 mL distilled water), and then the assembly of CQDs on CuBi2O4 followed the procedure as described in Section 2.1. The flow chart for the synthesis of the CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite is schematically shown in Fig. 1(II).

To obtain the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite photocatalyst, 1 g of the as-prepared CQDs/CuBi2O4 was loaded in 30 mL distilled water, followed by adding 0.1689 g (1 mmol) AgNO3 under magnetic stirring for 1 h. 0.1201 g (0.5 mmol) of Na2S was dissolved in 20 mL of distilled water, which was then dropped slowly to the above CQDs/CuBi2O4 suspension. The reaction was conducted for 4 h under magnetic stirring, during which Ag2S quantum dots were grown on the surface of CQDs/CuBi2O4. The product was collected, washed with distilled water and absolute ethanol, and dried in a thermostat drier at 60 °C for 5 h. The flow chart for the synthesis of the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite is shown in Fig. 1(III). It is noted that the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite prepared according to the above procedure has a Ag2S mass fraction of 11%, and this composite is confirmed to be the optimal composite sample.

The phase purity of the CuBi2O4, CQDs/CuBi2O4, and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 photocatalysts was analyzed by means of X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation at a wavelength of 1.5406 Å operated at 40 kV and 30 mA. The surface morphology of as-prepared samples was characterized by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) diffuse reflectance spectra of the samples were examined on a UV–vis spectrophotometer with an integrating sphere attachment using BaSO4 as the reference. To determine the bonding states of the samples, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed by using a PHI-5702 multi-functional x-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The Brunauer Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas of the samples were characterized by N2 adsorption-desorption technique using a Tristar II 3020 porosimetry analyzer.

The photoelectrochemical properties of the CuBi2O4, CQDs/CuBi2O4, and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 photocatalysts were investigated on a CST 350 electrochemical workstation using a three-electrode cell configuration as described in the literature [52]. In this experiment, the photocatalysts, standard calomel electrode (SCE), and Pt foil electrode were used as the working electrode, reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively. The working electrode included 15 mg of the photocatalysts, 0.75 mg of polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), 0.75 mg of carbon black, and 1 mL of 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidione (NMP). The formed slurry was evenly dispersed on the fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass substrate with an effective area of 1 cm × 1 cm and transferred to the 60 °C thermostat drying oven for 5 h. In all photoelectrochemical measurements, 0.1 mol L−1 Na2SO4 aqueous solution (pH = 7) was applied as the electrolyte and simulated sunlight emitted from a 200 W xenon lamp was used as the light source. The sunlight simulator has a wavelength region of 300–2500 nm and total irradiance of 0.58 W cm−1 at a distance of 1 cm. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data were measured by imposing a small sinusoidal voltage of 5 mV amplitude in the frequency range from 10−2 to 105 Hz. The transient photocurrent response spectra were measured at a bias potential of 0.2 V under intermittent illumination of simulated sunlight.

The degradation experiments of the CuBi2O4, CQDs/CuBi2O4, and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 photocatalysts were performed by the degradation of RhB dye under irradiation of a 200 W xenon lamp emitting simulated sunlight. The initial RhB concentration was 5 mg L−1 and the photocatalyst loading was 1 g L−1 (i.e., 0.1 g photocatalyst was added in 100 mL RhB solution). Before illumination, the mixed solution was magnetically stirred for 30 min in the dark to achieve adsorption equilibrium between RhB dye and photocatalyst. After that, 1 mL of H2O2 was added to the mixed solution and then initiate the photocatalytic reaction under irradiation of a 200 W xenon lamp. During the degradation experiment, the water-jacketed reactor was cooled with water-cooling system to keep the mixed solution at room temperature. Subsequently, a small amount of the solution was taken, after the elapse of a period of time, for examination of the RhB dye concentration. The concentration of RhB dye at intervals of 15 min was determined by measuring the absorbance of the solution at a fixed wavelength of λ = 554 nm using a UV–vis spectrophotometer, where the photocatalyst was removed by centrifugation at 4000 r min−1 for 10 min. The degradation percentage of the dye is defined as (C 0 − C t)/C 0 × 100%, where C 0 and C t are the dye concentrations before and after t min of photocatalysis, respectively. To study the reactive species, the effect of ethanol, benzoquinone (BQ) and ammonium oxalate (AO) on the degradation of RhB over the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite was measured. Ethanol, BQ and AO were applied as the scavengers of hydroxyl (
OH), superoxide (
O2 −) and h+, respectively [15]. Before the photocatalytic reaction, 5 mL of ethanol, 0.1 mmol of BQ and 0.1 mmol of AO were separately added in 100 mL reaction solution. Subsequently, the degradation experiment was performed under the identical conditions as described above.

The crystallisation process of the CuBi2O4 precursor was studied by XRD. Fig. 2 shows the XRD patterns of CuBi2O4 samples obtained at different reaction times (0.5, 1, 3, 7 and 12 h). The samples derived at reaction times of 0.5 and 1 h contain the mixture phases, including tetragonal structure of BiOCl (PDF#06-0249) and CuBi2O4 tetragonal phase (PDF#42-0334). By increasing the reaction time up to 3 h, the BiOCl diffraction peaks disappear, and all the diffraction peaks can be indexed according to the CuBi2O4 tetragonal phase. When the reaction time continuously increases to 7 and 12 h, the obtained CuBi2O4 samples show similar XRD diffraction peaks in the XRD patterns. The result indicates that the pure tetragonal structure of CuBi2O4 is completely formed at 3 h and undergoes no change at longer reaction times.

Fig. 3 shows the SEM images of CuBi2O4 samples obtained at different reaction times. Fig. 3(a) shows the SEM image of the 0.5 h derived sample, which reveals that some irregular particles and flower-like embryos exist in the sample. Fig. 3(b) represents the SEM image of the sample with 1 h reaction time. It is observed that the sample manifests a morphology of flower-like clusters. The SEM image of Fig. 3(c) shows the formation of bundles of leeks assembled by nanosticks at reaction time of 7 h. As seen from the SEM image of Fig. 3(d), dumbbell-like architecture built from nanorods is formed in sample with reaction time of 12 h. It can be seen that the reaction time has an important effect on the morphology of the resultant CuBi2O4 samples.

Fig. 4 shows the growth process of dumbbell-like CuBi2O4 architecture and the formation mechanism of CQDs/CuBi2O4 and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composites. Initially, the formation of tetragonal BiOCl phase (see Fig. 2) results in irregular morphology of the target product. This period is the time of flower-like embryos formation. With increasing the reaction time, BiOCl disappears and CuBi2O4 tetragonal phase is crystallized. The formed CuBi2O4 dominates the formation of the flower-like clusters. When the reaction time reaches 7 h, CuBi2O4 nanosticks are grown and assembled into clusters of stacked sticks that eventually resemble bundles of leeks. Due to the continuous growth of the nanosticks at both ends, more thicker nanorods are formed and eventually assembled into the dumbbell-shaped architecture. The photocatalytic activity of CuBi2O4 may be enhanced by assembling CQDs on the dumbbell-shaped architecture. Ag2S is an efficient photocatalyst material and can act as a co-catalyst to enhance the photocatalytic performance when combined with other semiconductor. On this basis, the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite photocatalyst with enhanced photocatalytic activity can be obtained by decorating Ag2S nanoparticles on the CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite.

Fig. 5 shows the XRD patterns of the CuBi2O4, CQDs/CuBi2O4, and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 photocatalysts. For the CuBi2O4 sample prepared by a simple coprecipitation route with 12 h reaction time, all XRD characteristic peaks can be ascribed to the tetragonal structure of CuBi2O4 with PDF#42-0334. The XRD diffraction peaks of the CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite are similar to those of pure CuBi2O4, suggesting that the host lattice of the CuBi2O4 tetragonal structure in the composite undergoes no change. For the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite, besides the characteristic peaks of tetragonal CuBi2O4 phase, the characteristic peaks of Ag2S are also found in the composite. The characteristic peaks of CQDs are not found in the composite, which may be due to the weak X-ray diffraction of CQDs and low doping concentration of CQDs in the composite. More detailed measurements of the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite will be performed using TEM and XPS in the following sections.

FTIR spectroscopy is an important way to detect the functional groups of the CuBi2O4, CQDs/CuBi2O4, and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 photocatalysts. Fig. 6 shows the FTIR spectra of the three samples. For all the samples, the absorption peaks observed at 2935, 2860, 1710, 1647, 1549, 1464, 1091, 794, 676, 601 and 435 cm−1 can be observed. The absorption band in the range from 1091 to 400 cm−1 can be assigned to CuBi2O4 [53]. In addition, the absorption peak at 1091 cm−1 can be also considered as oxygen deficiency [54]. The absorption band in the range from 1710 to1464 cm−1 can be ascribed to the OH bond stretching and stretching vibration of BiO bonds [55]. The absorption peaks at 2935 and 2860 cm−1 were also observed by Najafian et al [53]. For CuBi2O4, other weak absorption peaks at 2611, 2435, 2271 and 2088 cm−1 can be ascribed to the oxygenated and/or organic nitrogen compounds [56]. For CQDs/CuBi2O4, four new absorption peaks at 2311, 1840, 1740 and 1324 cm−1 can be observed, which are ascribed to the CQDs [57]. For Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4, two new absorption peaks at 2327 and 578 cm−1 can be found, which are assigned to the NO2 − and AgS bond [58,59]. The result indicates that Ag2S and CQDs are successfully assembled onto the surface of CuBi2O4.

To further confirm the existence of CQDs in the CQDs/CuBi2O4 photocatalyst, Fig. 7 shows the Raman spectra of the CuBi2O4 and CQDs/CuBi2O4. For CuBi2O4, five Raman bands centered at 113, 176, 245, 384 and 563 cm−1 can be observed, which can be attributed to the translational vibration of the CuO4 plane along the Z-axis, the vibration of Cu–Cu, the rotation of two stacked CuO4 squares in opposite directions, the BiO stretching vibration and the in-plane breathing of CuO4 squares, respectively [60]. For the CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite, additional bands at 1489 and 1618 cm−1 are obviously detected, which are assigned to the D and G bands, respectively [61,62]. This result confirms the decoration of CQDs on the surface of CuBi2O4 architectures. The Raman bands of CuBi2O4 in the composite are weak and shift slightly towards to the shorter wavenumber could be due to the coverage of CQDs on the surface of the CuBi2O4.

The microstructure of the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite was studied by TEM, as shown in Fig. 8 . Fig. 8(a) shows the TEM image of the composite and Fig. 8(b) displays the local enlarged TEM image from the boxed region. The dumbbell-like architecture can be ascribed to CuBi2O4, whereas the fine particles can be assigned to Ag2S, as shown in Fig. 8(b). The inset in Fig. 8(b) shows the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern taken from the composite. Polycrystalline diffraction rings are clearly observed in the SAED pattern, which can be indexed into the (200), (211), (202) and (411) planes of CuBi2O4 and (121) plane of Ag2S. Fig. 8(c) shows the high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite. It is clearly seen that smaller-sized Ag2S particles are decorated on larger-sized CuBi2O4 crystals, both of which manifest perfect crystal lattice fringes. The lattice fringes with d-spacing of 0.318 nm correspond to the (211) plane of CuBi2O4, and the lattice fringes with d-spacing of 0.244 nm correspond to the (121) plane of Ag2S. Fig. 8(d) shows the dark field scanning TEM (DF-STEM) image of the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite. Fig. 8(e)‒(j) display the corresponding elemental maps of the selected region in Fig. 8(d). It is observed that the Cu, Bi, O, Ag, S and C elements are uniformly distributed throughout the composite. This result indicates that Ag2S nanoparticles and CQDs are uniformly assembled onto CuBi2O4 dumbbell-like architecture without obvious aggregation. The elemental composition of the composite can be further analyzed by the energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), as shown in Fig. 8(k). It can be seen that the elemental composition of the composite consists of the elements Cu, Bi, O, C, Ag and S. The observation of extremely high signal intensity of C and Cu could be ascribed to the additional contribution of carbon-coated Cu grids used in the TEM experiments [52].

N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm of the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite is plotted in Fig. 9 , and the pore size distribution curve of the composite acquired from the desorption branch of the isotherm using the Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) method is shown as the inset. The isotherm is type-II isotherm according to the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) classification [63], suggesting that the composite sample possesses mesopores structure with pore diameter of about 20 nm. The BET specific surface area of the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite is found to be 3.37 m2 g−1.

The color and optical properties of the CuBi2O4, CQDs/CuBi2O4, and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 photocatalysts were investigated by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy. Fig. 10 (a) shows the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the samples. For CuBi2O4 and CQDs/CuBi2O4, the two samples present higher reflectance in the wavelength region from 620 to 850 nm. When Ag2S is introduced to CQDs/CuBi2O4, a decrease in the reflectance of the resultant Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite is observed. The color parameters (L*, a*, b*), chroma parameter (c*), hue angle (Ho), and total colour difference (ECIE *) of CuBi2O4, CQDs/CuBi2O4 and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 are calculated on the basis of literatures [64,65] and the obtained results are given in Table 1 . The CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite shows the largest L* and b* values, suggesting that it displays a light yellow color, as shown in Fig. 10(c). It can be also seen that the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite exhibits the smallest a* value and relatively small b* value, which means the composite exhibits yellowish black, as shown in Fig. 10(d). For bare CuBi2O4, the color is between yellow and yellowish black, as shown in Fig. 10(b). The result also indicates that bare CuBi2O4 is a potential pigment. The optical energy gap (E g) of the CuBi2O4, CQDs/CuBi2O4, and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 photocatalysts can be also obtained on the basis of the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy. The inset in Fig. 10(a) shows the first derivative curves of the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the samples. It is seen that CuBi2O4, CQDs/CuBi2O4, and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 exhibit absorption edges at 657.4, 657.4 and 645.4 nm, respectively, indicating that the Eg values of CuBi2O4 in the three samples are about 1.886, 1.886 and 1.921 eV, respectively.

X-ray photoelectron spectrometer was used to study the chemical composition and electronic core levels of the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite. As can be seen from the XPS survey scan spectrum in Fig. 11 (a), the composite clearly contains the Cu, Bi, O, C, Ag and S elements. The electronic core levels of Cu 2p, Bi 4f, O 1s, C 1s and Ag 3d in Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 were further studied by the high resolution XPS spectra. Fig. 11(b) shows the Cu 2p core-level XPS spectrum. Four obvious peaks at 934.6, 942.8, 954.8 and 963.1 eV are observed on the spectrum. The peaks at 934.6 and 954.8 eV are assigned to Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 binding energies of CuBi2O4, respectively [66,67]. The two satellite peaks at 942.8 and 963.1 eV can be attributed to the Cu2+ oxidation state [66,67]. Fig. 11(c) displays the XPS spectrum of Bi 4f core level. The peaks at 159.0 and 164.4 eV are ascribed to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, respectively, indicating that Bi species exhibits +3 oxidation state [66,67]. As shown in Fig. 11(d), the O 1s core-level XPS spectrum can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 530.1 and 531.7 eV, which are assigned to the lattice oxygen in CuBi2O4 and adsorbed oxygen, respectively [68–70]. Fig. 11(e) shows the C 1s core-level XPS spectrum. The C 1s peaks at 284.8, 283.2 and 280.3 eV can be attributed to the adventitious hydrocarbon for the XPS instruments [69], CQDs [71] and impurity structure of carbon [72]. On the Ag 3d core-level XPS spectrum (Fig. 11(f)), two peaks at 368.2 and 374.2 eV are observed, which are ascribed to Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2, respectively [73,74]. This implies that Ag species exists in Ag+ oxidation state. In addition, it is noteworthy that the binding energy peaks of S 2p overlap largely with those of Bi 4f [73], and moreover, their intensity is much smaller than that of Bi 4f peaks due to the low content of Ag2S in the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite. As a result, it is difficult to separate S 2p signals from Bi 4f peaks by deconvolution.

EIS and transient photocurrent response were used to understand the charge transfer process and photoelectrochemical activity of the CuBi2O4, CQDs/CuBi2O4, and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 photocatalysts. Fig. 12 (a) shows the photocurrent response curves of the samples. Based on the literature [75], the photocurrent response process can be used to characterize the photoelectrochemical activity of the photocatalysts. The photocurrent intensity of the samples follows the order: Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 > CQDs/CuBi2O4 > CuBi2O4. The enhanced photocurrent for CQDs/CuBi2O4 can be ascribed to the electron transfer between CQDs and CuBi2O4. For Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4, besides the electron transfer between CQDs and CuBi2O4, more important Z-scheme electron transfer between Ag2S and CuBi2O4 takes place. As a result, the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite has the highest separation efficiency of electrons and holes and thus manifests the strongest photocurrent intensity. This result implies that the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite possesses the highest photocatalytic activity for degradation of organic pollutants. The EIS spectra of the CuBi2O4, CQDs/CuBi2O4, and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 photocatalysts under simulated sunlight irradiation are shown in Fig. 12(b). For the three samples, a semicircle appears in the high frequencies, which can be assigned to the charge transfer occurring in the electrode/electrolyte interface [76,77]. The smallest semicircle radius also implies that the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite exhibits the fastest interfacial charge transfer, and then is expected to manifest the highest photocatalytic activity.

To study the photocatalytic activity of the CuBi2O4, CQDs/CuBi2O4, and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 photocatalysts, Fig. 13 (a) shows the time-dependent photocatalytic degradation of RhB over the photocatalysts under simulated sunlight irradiation. In the absence of the photocatalysts, RhB dye is almost stable and difficult to be degraded under simulated solar irradiation [78]. As can be seen from Fig. 13(a), the adsorption percentage of RhB onto the CuBi2O4, CQDs/CuBi2O4, and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 photocatalysts is about 6.9%, 11.1%, and 11.9%, respectively. Compared to bare CuBi2O4, the composites manifest a slightly increased adsorption toward RhB. Generally an appropriate increase in the dye adsorption is conducive to the photocatalysis. After 60 min of the photocatalytic reaction, the degradation percentage of RhB over the CuBi2O4, CQDs/CuBi2O4, and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 photocatalysts is found to be 85.6%, 90.6%, and 99.3%, respectively. This result indicates that the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite exhibits the highest photocatalytic activity, which is consistent with the predicted result by electrochemical test.

The first-order kinetic rate of the CuBi2O4, CQDs/CuBi2O4, and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 photocatalysts for the degradation of RhB dye was evaluated on the basis of literature [79]. Fig. 13(b) shows the plots of Ln(Ct/C0) vs. irradiation time t. From the slope of the regression lines, the rate constant k for CuBi2O4, CQDs/CuBi2O4 and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 is obtained to be 0.03345, 0.03982 and 0.08558 min−1, respectively. This suggests that the CQDs/CuBi2O4 and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composites exhibit a photocatalytic activity about 1.2 and 2.6 times higher than that of bare CuBi2O4, respectively. Fig. 13(c) shows the cycling stability of the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 photocatalyst for the degradation of RhB dye for 4 successive cycles. After four cycles, no obvious decrease in the dye degradation is observed, which implies that the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 photocatalyst exhibits a high stability. To exclude the photosensitization effect of RhB, the colourless pollutant, phenol, was also selected as the model to investigate the photocatalytic performance of CuBi2O4, CQDs/CuBi2O4, and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 (Fig. S1). It is seen that all the samples also exhibit pronounced photocatalytic activity toward the phenol degradation, and moreover, an enhanced photocatalytic activity is observed for the composites, particularly Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4.

According to literature [15], the active species including
OH,
O2 − and photogenerated h+ can be detected by ethanol, BQ and AO. Fig. 13(d) shows the effects of ethanol, BQ and AO on the degradation of the RhB dye over the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 photocatalyst under simulated sunlight irradiation. The result shows that the photocatalytic reaction is significantly suppressed by the addition of ethanol or BQ. However, the addition of AO to the photocatalytic system leads to only a slight suppression of the dye degradation. This suggests that
OH and
O2 − play the key role in the dye degradation.

Based on the aforementioned experimental results and analyses, a possible photocatalytic mechanism is proposed to elucidate the photocatalytic degradation of the RhB dye over the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite as shown in Fig. 14 . The photocatalytic activity is relevant to the electron transfer process as well as light absorption ability, band edge position, separation and utilization efficiency of photogenerated carriers [67]. Under simulated sunlight irradiation, electrons are excited from the valence band (VB) of CuBi2O4 and Ag2S separately to their conduction band (CB). Simultaneously the electronic excitation process also occurs in CQDs, i.e., electrons in the π orbital or σ orbital of CQDs are excited to a high-energy state such as the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) [80]. The photoexcited CQDs are excellent electron donors and electron acceptors. As a result, the CB electrons in CuBi2O4 will transfer to the π orbital or σ orbital of CQDs. Conversely the photoexcited electrons in CQDs can be also transferred to the CB of CuBi2O4. The interesting electron transfer process can efficiently suppress the recombination of photogenerated electron/hole pairs in CuBi2O4. On the other hand, the electron transfer process also takes place between CuBi2O4 and Ag2S. According to previously reported results, the CB and VB potentials of CuBi2O4 are −0.36 and +1.53 V vs normal hydrogen electrode (NHE), respectively [29], whereas Ag2S has a VB potential of +0.70 V vs NHE and a CB potential more negative than −0.36 V vs NHE due to its quantum effect [46]. The well-matched band edge positions suggest that Z-scheme Ag2S/CuBi2O4 heterojunction is expected to be formed in the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite. According to the literature [81], a similar phenomenon can be also observed for other semiconductors. As a result, the photogenerated electrons in the CB of CuBi2O4 will transfer to Ag2S and recombine with the photogenerated holes in the VB of Ag2S. This Z-scheme electron transfer process significantly suppresses the electron/hole pair recombination. In addition, CQDs also play a key role as the charge transmission bridge for the photocatalytic reactions. The photoexcited electrons in the CB of CuBi2O4 will migrate to CQDs, and subsequently transfer to Ag2S, which is helpful for the Z-scheme electron transfer process [82]. All these collectively suggest that the Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite manifests significantly enhanced photocatalytic activity toward the dye degradation.

It is noted that the VB potential of CuBi2O4 is not positive than the redox potentials of OH−/
OH (+1.99 vs NHE) and H2O/
OH (+2.38 V vs NHE) [68], suggesting that the photogenerated h+ cannot thermodynamically react with OH− or H2O to produce
OH. According to the literature [29], the generation of
OH is highly dependent on
O2 −, implying that
OH is mainly generated from the reaction between
O2 − and H2O2. This reaciton process also suggests that the addition of H2O2 can greatly promote the
OH generation. On the other hand, the generation of
O2 − could be derived from the reaction of adsorbed O2 molecules with the photoexcited electrons in CQDs or CB electrons in CuBi2O4 and Ag2S. The main reactions can be briefly described as follows.
(1)
e − + O2 →
O2 −
(2)
O2 − + H2O2 →
OH + OH− + O2
(3)
OH + dye → degradation products

A simple coprecipitation route was used to synthesize CuBi2O4 dumbbell-like architecture built from nanorods and the growth process of the dumbbell-like architecture was investigated by varying the reaction duration time. The phase transformation sequence of the precursor with increasing the reaction time is determined as BiOCl(major) + CuBi2O4(minor) → BiOCl(minor) + CuBi2O4(major) → CuBi2O4. CQDs/CuBi2O4 and Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composites were prepared by decorating CQDs and Ag2S nanoparticles on the surface of the CuBi2O4 dumbbell-like architecture. The photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared samples follows the order: Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 > CQDs/CuBi2O4 > CuBi2O4. The Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 composite exhibits the highest photocatalytic activity toward the RhB degradation, which can be attributed to the highly efficient separation of electron/hole pairs and increased visible light absorption of the composite. The active species tests indicate that
O2 − and
OH play a key role in the photocatalytic reaction system.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51662027), Gansu education department higher school research project (2018A-242) and HongLiu First-Class Disciplines Development Program of Lanzhou University of Technology.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
